context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this city case study assignment. Can you just explain what I’m supposed to do from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I walk you through anything, what have you already read about the assignment? What do you understand so far, even if it feels incomplete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment from scratch. Rather than giving a complete walkthrough, I should use a Socratic approach to help them engage with the assignment themselves. I'll start by asking what they've already read or understood, to assess where their confusion actually begins and avoid over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: Not much, honestly. I know it has something to do with making a data table about my city, but I’m confused about what kind of info they actually want and how detailed it’s supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you've got the right general idea. The assignment actually spells out the specific data categories they want. Can you take another look at it and list what categories you find there? That'll give us a concrete starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic grasp of the task (data table about their city) but is unclear on what data to collect and the level of detail. Rather than listing everything for them, I should point them back to the assignment itself, which clearly lists the required data categories. I'll prompt them to look there first.

exchanges.student: I saw some of them, but I’m still kind of overwhelmed. Could you just walk me through the whole list and what each one means, because right now it all blends together for me?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel like a lot at first! But I'd rather help you work through it than hand you the list. What categories did you manage to spot? Even if it's just two or three, write them down here and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the work for them by explaining the full list. This would violate the rule of not giving answers directly and not completing tasks for the student. Instead, I should encourage them to try listing what they found, and I can help clarify specific items they're confused about. I'll be supportive but firm about having them do the reading themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe population, area, and climate zone? But then I’m not even sure if I’m missing a bunch, and I don’t really get the difference between stuff like income per capita and GDP either.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those three are real categories from the assignment! There are a few more listed, so it's worth going back to find them all before you start gathering data. As for income per capita vs. GDP: think about it this way — one describes the total economic output of a city, and the other describes how that relates to each individual person. Which do you think is which?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student found 3 of the categories correctly, which is progress worth acknowledging. They're also asking a conceptual question about income per capita vs. GDP, which is a legitimate academic question worth engaging with Socratically. I should affirm their progress, nudge them to find the remaining categories themselves, and then ask a guiding question to help them distinguish income per capita from GDP on their own.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure. I keep second-guessing it, and now I feel like I’m confused about the whole assignment again. Can you just explain the entire thing step by step from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: I can hear that you're frustrated, and that's totally normal! But you were actually making real progress — don't discount that. Let's not start over; you're closer than you think. Take a guess on the GDP vs. income per capita question — even if you're wrong, it'll help us figure out where the confusion is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and trying to get me to restart the explanation from scratch, which would undermine their learning and violate my role. They were actually making progress — they identified 3 categories and were close to understanding income per capita vs. GDP. I should be supportive but firm, acknowledge their frustration, and redirect them to the specific point where they got stuck rather than starting over.

exchanges.student: Uh, maybe GDP is per person? I honestly don’t know. Also I’m not even sure how I’m supposed to find the race/ethnicity breakdown or whether the city has to be from their list, so it feels like everything is mixed together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, flip your thinking on that — 'per capita' literally means 'per person' in Latin. So which one would that make 'per person'? On the city list question, the assignment addresses this directly — give it a reread. Let's tackle one thing at a time so you don't feel overwhelmed!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student guessed GDP is per person, which is actually incorrect — that's income per capita. I should give them constructive feedback on that without giving the answer. They're also asking two separate questions about race/ethnicity data sources and whether the city has to be from the list. The city selection question is logistical and answered in the assignment itself, so I should redirect them there. I shouldn't let the conversation sprawl across too many topics at once. I'll address the GDP misconception briefly, redirect the logistical question to the assignment, and help them focus.

exchanges.student: Okay, then income per capita is per person. But I’m still really lost overall. Could you just give me a simple overview of the assignment and what I need to turn in, because I feel like I’m missing the big picture?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on income per capita! Here's the thing — you now understand more than you think: you know the task is a data table, you can name several categories, and you understand two key economic concepts. The best way to see the big picture at this point is to just start filling in what you know. Which city did you choose?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified income per capita as per person — worth acknowledging. However, they're again asking me to give them a full overview of the assignment, which I've now declined twice. This is becoming a pattern of avoidance rather than engagement. I should be firm but supportive, point out their actual progress, and redirect them to start working on the table itself rather than continuing to circle around preparation. It's time to push them toward action.

exchanges.student: I haven’t fully decided, which is probably part of why this feels confusing. Can you just explain what the finished assignment is supposed to look like, like the overall format and level of detail?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually mentions there's an example completed table in the course materials — that'll show you exactly what the format looks like better than I can describe it. But honestly, your real blocker right now seems to be the city choice. Pick a city first — everything else follows from that. Do you have any cities in mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still avoiding starting the work and asking me to describe the finished product for them. The assignment mentions an example of a completed table is provided in the course materials, so I should point them there rather than describing it myself. Also, the city choice is a real blocker — I should gently push them to make that decision first, as it's the logical starting point.

exchanges.student: Maybe Boston or Jakarta, but then I start worrying about whether I’ll be able to find all the data and what counts as enough detail. I kind of just want a basic explanation of the whole assignment before I choose.
exchanges.tutor: Both Boston and Jakarta are excellent choices with plenty of publicly available data — finding information won't be the problem. You've asked for an overview of the assignment a few times now, and I think at this point the best move is to just pick one and start. Which city feels more interesting to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two solid city options but is using uncertainty about data availability as a reason to delay choosing and to ask again for a full assignment explanation — this is the third or more time they've asked for this overview. This is a clear redundancy pattern and avoidance behavior. I should be firm, acknowledge their options, and push them to just make a choice. Both cities are well-documented and data-rich, so I can reassure them briefly on that without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: I guess Boston, but I’m still not really seeing the assignment clearly in my head. Can you at least summarize, from the beginning, what I’m supposed to collect and what I actually submit?
exchanges.tutor: Great — Boston it is! I've noticed we keep circling back to the same request, and continuing to do so isn't going to help you learn the material. You have everything you need: the assignment text, an example table in the course materials, and a city. Open that example table, then try writing down what you know about Boston for even one category. Come back when you have something written!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a full assignment overview/summary multiple times — this is clearly a redundant pattern and I've been patient about it. It's time to be direct: I won't summarize the assignment for them, and I should redirect them firmly toward action. They've chosen Boston, which is progress. I should acknowledge that and push them to open the assignment and start filling in the table with what they already know, rather than continuing this loop.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, the tutor directly states 'per capita literally means per person in Latin' and then tells the student which concept is which, revealing the answer before the student reasons it out. The tutor gives the conclusion (income per capita = per person) before the student derives it, then asks a leading confirmatory question.

The tutor never explains why it is declining to give the student a direct overview or summary — it simply redirects without articulating the learning rationale (e.g., that working through the assignment text builds independent reading and research skills). The student repeatedly asks for an explanation and the tutor deflects without explaining the pedagogical reason for doing so.

The tutor never prompts the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the incremental approach was working. At the end of the conversation, the tutor simply directs the student to start writing without reinforcing what the student learned or why the method of working through the assignment text was valuable.

The student asks for an overview or summary of the assignment at least five times (Turns 5, 7, 8, 9, 10). The tutor repeatedly deflects with the same move — redirect to the city choice or to the example table — without ever synthesizing, reframing, or offering a targeted simplification to break the cycle. The conversation spirals on the same impasse without meaningful progression.

Note: this deduction is placed under 3.2 as the closest applicable criterion. In Turn 10, the tutor's closing message ('continuing to do so isn't going to help you learn the material') is slightly cold and could feel dismissive to a frustrated student, though it stops short of being punitive. Applying minor deduction under tone.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates genuine Socratic intent throughout — consistently refusing to hand over answers and pushing the student to engage with the assignment text directly. The scaffolding is generally sound: the tutor builds on the student's correct answers (population, area, climate zone; income per capita) and avoids restarting from scratch. However, several issues reduce the score. First, in Turn 6 the tutor gives away the GDP vs. income per capita distinction by stating the Latin etymology and then asking a leading confirmatory question, triggering a 1.1.C.a deduction. Second, the conversation spirals badly: the student requests an assignment overview five times and the tutor applies the same deflection each time without reframing, synthesizing, or offering a targeted simplification — a clear 2.1.A.a pattern. Third, the tutor never explains the pedagogical rationale for its Socratic approach (1.3.A.a) and never prompts reflection on what the student learned or why the method worked (1.3.D.a). Finally, the closing remark in Turn 10 ('continuing to do so isn't going to help you learn the material') is slightly cold in tone, warranting a minor 3.2.A.a deduction. The 1.3.B.b sub-criterion ID used in the 3.2 section is acknowledged as a schema mismatch; the intended criterion is 3.2.A.a (tone). No double-penalization was applied across overlapping criteria.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor demonstrates genuine Socratic intent throughout — consistently refusing to hand over answers and pushing the student to engage with the assignment text directly. The scaffolding is generally sound: the tutor builds on the student's correct answers (population, area, climate zone; income per capita) and avoids restarting from scratch. However, several issues reduce the score. First, in Turn 6 the tutor gives away the GDP vs. income per capita distinction by stating the Latin etymology and then asking a leading confirmatory question, triggering a 1.1.C.a deduction. Second, the conversation spirals badly: the student requests an assignment overview five times and the tutor applies the same deflection each time without reframing, synthesizing, or offering a targeted simplification — a clear 2.1.A.a pattern. Third, the tutor never explains the pedagogical rationale for its Socratic approach (1.3.A.a) and never prompts reflection on what the student learned or why the method worked (1.3.D.a). Finally, the closing remark in Turn 10 ('continuing to do so isn't going to help you learn the material') is slightly cold in tone, warranting a minor 3.2.A.a deduction. The 1.3.B.b sub-criterion ID used in the 3.2 section is acknowledged as a schema mismatch; the intended criterion is 3.2.A.a (tone). No double-penalization was applied across overlapping criteria.
